ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for soy-marinated soft-boiled egg. FOODON_03301710 (hen egg food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers hen eggs as a category. No specific code exists for marinated or soft-boiled preparations. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 102263004 represents eggs as a food concept. No specific code for soy-marinated soft-boiled egg exists; closest parent used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is one whole soy-marinated soft-boiled egg, approximately 55 g, as typically served at Korean restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Egg, whole, cooked, hard-boiled (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, NDB 01129) adapted with soy marinade adjustment
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for whole cooked egg (hard-boiled, NDB 01129 / FDC ID 173424). Soft-boiled preparation yields marginally lower water loss than hard-boiled; macros are treated as equivalent per 100 g edible portion. Sodium is adjusted upward to approximately 400 mg per 100 g to reflect soy-sauce marinade absorption, consistent with published estimates for soy-marinated eggs in Korean culinary literature and USDA soy sauce sodium data (USDA FDC ID 172238). The adjustment is documented as an estimate.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Egg and egg-based foods
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for soy-marinated soft-boiled egg. Whole cooked eggs have a GI of approximately 13 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 (Am J Clin Nutr). The soy marinade contributes negligible additional carbohydrate per serving. GI of 13 and low category applied by analogy. Glycemic load per 55 g serving = (13 × 1.54 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 0.2.

hypertension.guidance: Each egg absorbs significant sodium from the soy marinade (estimated 220 mg per 55 g serving); individuals with hypertension should limit intake to one egg per meal and monitor total daily sodium against the recommended ceiling of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index and minimal carbohydrate content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 13 and only 1.5 g of available carbohydrate per serving, soy-marinated soft-boiled egg has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal; however, the high cholesterol content (approximately 205 mg per egg) warrants consideration in the context of individual lipid management plans.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Dietary cholesterol and sodium load from soy marinade
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: One soy-marinated egg provides approximately 205 mg of dietary cholesterol and 220 mg of sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume eggs in moderation consistent with current dietary guidelines (up to one egg per day for most adults) and be mindful of cumulative sodium intake from soy-based preparations.

llm_training.natural_language_description: A soy-marinated soft-boiled egg, known colloquially in Singapore as a 'Lava Egg', is a Korean-style preparation in which a whole hen egg is soft-boiled to achieve a fully set white and a semi-liquid, jammy yolk — the 'lava' centre — before being steeped in a seasoned soy-based marinade typically composed of soy sauce, water, sugar, garlic, and sesame oil. The dish is a staple accompaniment to Korean ramyeon (instant noodle soup) and bibimbap, and is widely served at Korean casual-dining restaurants in Singapore such as Ajumma's. The egg absorbs the umami-rich, slightly sweet and savoury marinade over several hours, resulting in a glossy, amber-coloured exterior and a deeply flavoured, silky interior. Nutritionally, one egg (approximately 55 g) provides around 85 kcal, 7 g of protein, 6 g of fat, and less than 2 g of carbohydrate, making it a protein-dense, low-carbohydrate food. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, which is elevated due to soy sauce absorption (estimated 220 mg per serving), and dietary cholesterol (approximately 205 mg per serving). The glycemic index is very low (approximately 13), making it suitable for blood glucose management. It is vegetarian, halal-friendly, and dairy-free, but contains egg, soy, and sesame allergens. In clinical nutrition contexts, it is a useful high-protein, low-GI food that requires sodium monitoring for hypertensive individuals.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a Korean soy egg?
Is a marinated soft-boiled egg healthy?
What is the sodium content of a soy egg?
What allergens are in a Korean soy egg?
How is a Korean ramen egg made?
How much protein is in a lava egg?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for soy-marinated soft-boiled egg exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy whole cooked egg data with a documented sodium adjustment for soy marinade absorption. GI is estimated by analogy from the University of Sydney GI Database whole egg value. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Sodium_mg is estimated at 400 mg per 100 g (approximately 220 mg per 55 g serving) to reflect soy-sauce marinade absorption; the base USDA value for plain hard-boiled egg is approximately 124 mg per 100 g. This adjustment is an estimate and should be validated against laboratory analysis of the specific restaurant preparation. 2. Trans_fat_g is set to 0.0; eggs contain negligible industrially produced trans fats — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. Vitamin_c_mg is set to 0.0; eggs contain no meaningful vitamin C — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 4. Dietary_fibre_g is set to 0.0; eggs contain no dietary fibre — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 5. Monounsaturated_fat_g (-1 sentinel not used; value 4.1 sourced from USDA FDC). Polyunsaturated_fat_g value 1.4 sourced from USDA FDC. 6. GI of 13 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database whole egg data; no direct measurement for soy-marinated preparation exists. 7. The food_name 'Soy-Marinated Soft-Boiled Egg' is used as the canonical English name; 'Lava Egg' is preserved as the primary alias reflecting the field survey data menu name. 8. Korean mandatory multilingual name 간장 계란 (Ganjang Gyeran) is provided per G6 and Q1 requirements.
